coredata.dc:title: Luminescent Zn-MOF@COF hybrid for selective decontamination of Cu(II) ions and methylene blue dye in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Ajay
Arya, Kushal
Mehra, Sanjay
Kumar, Arvind
Kumar Mehta, Surinder
Kataria, Ramesh
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have attained great attraction from researchers nowadays because of their diverse applications owing to their tunable porosity and controllable functions. This work reports an interlinked hybrid composite of Zn-based MOF and imine-base COF (Zn-MOF@COF) based on the ZnN bond. The resulting mesoporous hybrid material shows excellent capability for detecting and removing Cu2+ ions and methylene blue (MB) from water. The adsorption process followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, and the Langmuir model fits for Cu2+ adsorption and the Freundlich isotherm model for MB adsorption. The thermodynamic studies showed that the reaction process between Zn-MOF@COF composite and adsorbates is endothermic. Further, negative ΔS0 show a decrease in randomness and an increase in the order of adsorbent on solid/liquid interface, and positive ΔG0 suggests non-spontaneous behaviour of the reaction process. The composite material detected Cu2+ ions via luminescence quenching with a high Ksv value and low detection limit, showing strong interactions with Cu2+. The maximum adsorption efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+ and MB is 99.8 and 97.6 %, respectively. The hybrid composite shows the best performance for decontaminating Cu2+ ions and MB among various COF- and MOF-based chemosensors.

head.ce:title: Luminescent Zn-MOF@COF hybrid for selective decontamination of Cu(II) ions and methylene blue dye in aqueous media

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly efficient luminescent Zn-MOF@COF hybrid has been successfully prepared and employed as specific detection and removal of Cu(II) ions and methylene blue dye in aqueous media with adsorption capability of 99.8 and 97.6 % respectively.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have attained great attraction from researchers nowadays because of their diverse applications owing to their tunable porosity and controllable functions. This work reports an interlinked hybrid composite of Zn-based MOF and imine-base COF (Zn-MOF@COF) based on the ZnN bond. The resulting mesoporous hybrid material shows excellent capability for detecting and removing Cu2+ ions and methylene blue (MB) from water. The adsorption process followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, and the Langmuir model fits for Cu2+ adsorption and the Freundlich isotherm model for MB adsorption. The thermodynamic studies showed that the reaction process between Zn-MOF@COF composite and adsorbates is endothermic.

Further, negative ΔS0 show a decrease in randomness and an increase in the order of adsorbent on solid/liquid interface, and positive ΔG0 suggests non-spontaneous behaviour of the reaction process. The composite material detected Cu2+ ions via luminescence quenching with a high Ksv value and low detection limit, showing strong interactions with Cu2+. The maximum adsorption efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+ and MB is 99.8 and 97.6 %, respectively. The hybrid composite shows the best performance for decontaminating Cu2+ ions and MB among various COF- and MOF-based chemosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: All living beings are currently suffering from serious problems caused by environmental pollution and the global energy crisis. The growing population and industrialisation have led to water contamination due to the addition of hazardous organic pollutants, including heavy metals, pesticides, antibiotics, dyes, etc., in water bodies.[1–3] Dyes are colourful chemical substances excessively used in different sectors such as textile, food and cosmetic industries for colouring purposes.[4] Most dye effluents are just dumped into water bodies without any treatment, which prevents sunlight penetration, obstructing photosynthesis and ruining the balance of the aquatic biosphere.[4,5] These organic pollutants also negatively affect human beings due to their potential toxicity, carcinogenic nature and non-biodegradable properties.[4–6] In particular, Methylene blue (MB) is one of the most commonly used dyes in textile industries for dyeing leather or tannin.[6,7] Being a cationic dye, it can easily interact with the anionic surface of the cell membrane to penetrate inside the cell.[4,8] The bioaccumulation of MB may cause biological toxicity hurting the metabolism of aquatic species, and also poses a severe threat to human health such as abdominal pain, nausea, and even cancer.[4,9,10] The removal of MB from water is quite challenging using conventional methods due to high water solubility and chemical stability.[11,12].

However, Cu is an essential biologically relevant metal involved in several biological processes at low concentrations.[13–15] However, excessive Cu concentration can cause several biological disorders in humans, including Wilson disease, neurodegenerative and metabolism disorders, cardiovascular diseases, oxidative stress, and hypertension.[16–19] The average amount of Cu in blood ranges from 15.7 to 23.6 μM[20], and the permissible concentration of Cu in drinking water is 20 μM as per WHO guidelines.[21] However, the Cu levels in water are much higher than the permissible amount because of industrialization, mining, and waste dumping in rivers.[22,23] Therefore, there is a need for a potential chemical tool that can detect and remove such types of water pollutants. As per the literature, many techniques for decontamination of heavy metal ions and organic dyes are available, including ultrafiltration,[24] chemical precipitation,[25] adsorption and membrane-based separation,[26] colorimetric,[27] absorbances,[28] ICP-MS,[3] fluorescence[29] and many more has been employed. Among all, the fluorescence method for recognizing heavy metal ions and the adsorption method for removing metal ions and organic dyes has been widely used due to high removal efficiency, low energy consumption, quick response, simple operation, and low cost.[4,5,30,31].

Over the past few years, Luminescent Metal-Organic Frameworks (LMOFs) and Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) are the most commonly used as chemical sensors and adsorbents for heavy metal ions and organic dyes because of their diverse characteristics.[5,32,33–37] For instance, Zr-MOF (UiO-66),[38] MOF-235,[39] Zn-MOF,[40] Cu-BTC[5] and many other LMOFs have been synthesized and available with ultrahigh removal capacities towards MB and Cu(II) due to high surface area, adsorption sites available on MOF for H-bonding or π-π interactions and most importantly the presence of pores having size fit for penetration of dye molecules. In addition, benzimidazole and 1,3,5-triazine-based COF materials have been utilized for the adsorption of MB with the highest adsorption capability of 63.29 and 45.45 mg g−1 respectively.[32,41,42] Moreover, other sensors such as metal oxides, quantum dots, nanoparticles, and organic ligands are available for the detection of metal ions and the adsorption of dyes. The available sensors have some constraints in terms of sensitivity, selective recognition, recyclability, and response time [43–49].

The MOF@COF-based hybrid materials can be promising candidates for decontaminating heavy metal ions and organic dyes. As per the literature, a few articles deal with the hybridisation of MOF and COF for the adsorption of organic pollutants. Recently, MOF-5/COF[50] and MIL-101-NH2@COF[51] hybrid materials have been synthesized and successfully utilized to remove rhodamine B, auramine O and acid blue 9 dyes. Suresh et al. have recently reported the adsorption of MB over MOF-COF composites. [42] and synthesized materials were highly resistant to acidic and alkaline mediums due to strong covalent and coordination bonding. The adsorption of MB was facilitated by H-bonding, π-π and electrostatic interactions with a recovery rate of adsorbent up to seven cycles. As per the best of our literature knowledge, The Zn-MOF@COF-based hybrid composite materials were explored for energy storage devices, catalysis and gas separation [52–58], but there is no MOF@COF composite available for decontamination of Cu2+ ions in water. Therefore, there is a lot of space for the design and synthesis of MOF@COF-based chemosensors for decontaminating water pollutants so that drinking water can be made pure and safe for human consumption.

Herein, we have prepared a Zn-MOF@COF composite by using imine-based COF and Zn-MOF to overcome the existing limitations in terms of selective recognition, sensitivity, recyclability and pH-independent detection and removal of Cu2+ and MB in aqueous phase. The COF has been synthesized from btc-NH-NH2 and terphthaldehyde while Zn-MOF has been prepared by a solvothermal method by using 2,6-naphthalene dicarboxylic acid and Zn(NO3)4·6H2O Then, the MOF was grown solvothermally on the surface of COF by taking COF and Zn-MOF precursors in an autoclave at 100 °C for 72 h. The obtained Zn-MOF@COF composite was characterised by techniques such as FTIR, PXRD, BET, SEM, XPS etc. The results revealed successful coordination between the central atom Zn of MOF and the N atom of COF, leading to the formation of a mesoporous Zn-MOF@COF composite. We sought to explore the applicability of Zn-MOF@COF as a chemosensor for detecting and removing water pollutants. The Zn-MOF@COF was selective and sensitive for Cu2+ ions and methylene blue dye among various heavy metal ions and organic dyes. The Zn-MOF@COF composite detected Cu2+ ions via fluorescence quenching with a high Ksv value i.e. 0.7 × 105 M−1 and a low detection limit. Further, we explored the removal ability of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ and MB from water. The experimental results revealed that the estimated values of maximum adsorption efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+ and MB are 500 mg/g and 26.3 mg/g, respectively. Both the Langmuir model for Cu2+ adsorption and the Freundlich isotherm model for MB adsorption fit the pseudo-first-order kinetics of the adsorption process. Moreover, Zn-MOF@COF can be used up to five times to remove Cu2+ ions and MB dye. The Zn-MOF@COF composite can be used as a potential tool for decontaminating Cu2+ ions and MB dye from water.

Materials and methods: The following items were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (USA): N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, terephthaldehyde, benxenetricarbonyltrichoride, 2,6-Naphthalenedicarboxylic acid, and hydrazine hydrate. Other chemicals were purchased locally and utilised right away. Several instrumental approaches characterisedthe synthesised Zn-MOF, COF, and Zn-MOF@COF. All of the fluorescence tests were conducted using the F-7000 FL spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer FTIR 2000 instrument. PXRD data were collected through a PANalytical X'PertPRO X-ray diffractometer with Cu-K radiation (=1.54059). SCXRDfor Zn-MOF were collected at room temperature by a Rigaku Super-Nova diffractometer outfitted with a micro-focus enclosed X-ray tube with Mo-K radiation (=0.71073) and a CCD HyPix3000 detector. Thermo Scientific's ESCALAB 250 Xi photoelectron analyzer was used for the XPS tests. A JEOL JSM 7100F scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used for morphological characterization.

Synthesis of Btc-NH-NH2: Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (2 g, 7.532 mmol) was dissolved in 30 ml ethanol and refluxed at 80 °C temperature with stirring at 250 rpm for 1 h. Then, hydrazine hydrate (1.7468 ml, 35.8 mmol) was added dropwise in 5 min and refluxed for 18 h at 80 °C with stirring. After cooling at room temperature, the mixture was filtered and washed with ethanol 3–4 times, and finally, a colorless solid powder of Btc-NH-NH2 was obtained after overnight heating at 90 °C in an oven. FTIR (cm−1): 3304 (s), 1647 (s), 1512 (s), 1314 (s), 1117 (m), 1004 (s), 917 (m), 733 (m), 681 (s).

Synthesis of COF: The prepared Btc-NH-NH2 (0.2 g, 0.789 mmol) and terephthaldehyde (0.1587 g, 1.1835 mmol) were immersed in 1,4-dioxane followed by sonication for 15 min. Then, the mixture was refluxed for 12 h at 120 °C with gentle stirring at 250 rpm. After 12 h of refluxing, stirring continued for another 6 h. Then, the reaction was stopped and kept undisturbed for 24 h to produce a crystalline solid. Finally, yellowish crystalline solid COF was obtained after filtration and washing with DMF and ethanol three times (Yield 92.8 %). FTIR (cm−1): 1647 (s), 1607 (sm), 1541 (m), 1503 (sm), 1434 (sm), 1335 (sm), 1246 (s), 1167 (sm), 1059 (m), 947 (m), 826 (m), 724 (m).

Synthesis of Zn-MOF: Naphthalene-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (0.3 g, 1.3876 mmol) and Zn(NO3)4·6H2O (0.5193 g, 2.0815 mmol) were dissolved in a mixture of DMF and methanol (3:1,v/v) separately. Then both solutions were mixed and stirred for 30 min at 250 rpm. The methanol was used to induce polarity that will enhance the crystalline properties of the material. Three to five drops of acetic acid were added while stirring to lower the pH and allow all precipitates to dissolve in the reaction, which facilitates the formation of appropriate crystals. Then the reaction mixture was transferred to a 25 ml stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 72 h. The reaction was allowed to cool at room temperature and the colorless block shaped cube-like crystals of Zn-MOF were obtained after filtration and washing with DMF and acetone 5 times. FTIR (cm−1): 1609 (s), 1400 (s), 1264 (m), 1188 (m), 1055 (sm), 1018 (s), 962 (sm), 869 (s), 709 (m), 594 (s).

Synthesis of Zn-MOF@COF: The prepared Zn-MOF and COF were activated in chloroform for 7 days with an exchange of fresh chloroform every 12 h followed by overnight drying. The activated COF (0.045 g) and Zn(NO3)4·6H2O (0.051 g, 0.2081) were dispersed in DMF and sonicated for 12 h followed by the addition of naphthalene-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (0.03 g, 0.1387 mmol). Then the mixture was transferred to a 25 ml stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 72 h. The reaction was allowed to cool at room temperature and a greyish solid was obtained after filtration and washing with warm DMF and methanol at least thrice. FTIR (cm−1): 1684 (sm), 1654 (sm), 1601 (s), 1560 (m), 1545 (s), 1491 (s), 1395 (s), 1356 (s), 1251 (m), 1191 (m), 1140 (sm), 928 (s), 783 (vs), 643 (s), 586 (s).

Fluorescence experiments of Zn-MOF@COF for detection of Cu(II): The as-synthesized Zn-MOF@COF (10 mg) were dispersed in 20 ml triple distilled water and sonicated for 6 h. The suspension was stored as the first stock solution of Zn-MOF@COF. Subsequently, from this stock solution, we took 1 ml and added 9 ml of water to get 10 ml of the second stock solution. A constant volume of a second stock solution of Zn-MOF@COF (100 μL) was taken in a quartz cuvette (1 × 1 cm) followed by the addition of 100 μM concentration of each metal ions and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7) was used to make a total 3 ml in the cuvette and fluorescence spectra were recorded. Furthermore, a kinetic study was performed using the Stern-Volmer equation Fo/F = 1 + Ksv[A], where Fo and F are the fluorescence intensity of Zn-MOF@COF before and after adding analytes, respectively. [A] represents the Cu(II) concentration and Ksv is the quenching constant.

Kinetic Study of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: The adsorption kinetic experiments were performed to find the mechanism of adsorption of MB and Cu2+ ions onto Zn-MOF@COF composite and to evaluate the effect of time on the adsorption rate. To carry out this experiment, 50 and 20 mg of Zn-MOF@COF composite were added to the 50 ml of the solutions with initial MB and Cu2+ concentrations of 10 ppm and 50 ppm respectively. Both solutions were slowly stirred at room temperature for 45 min. During this process, 5 ml samples of the solution were collected at a fixed time interval of 5 min and the solid adsorbent material was removed from the samples with the help of a centrifuge machine. The UV–visible absorbance spectra and ICP-MS techniques were used to calculate the concentrations of MB and Cu2+ ions respectively that remained in the solution after the adsorption process. The adsorption capacity (q) and removal efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF was calculated using equation (1) and (2).

Kid stands for intraparticle diffusion rate constant (mg g−1 min1/2), and I represent the thickness of the boundary layer.

Isotherm study of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: The adsorbate's starting concentrations impact the adsorption effectiveness of an adsorbent. To determine the effect of initial concentrations of MB and Cu2+ ions on the removal efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF composite, the adsorption experiments were carried out by adding 5 and 2 mg of Zn-MOF@COF in 5 ml solutions of various concentrations of MB (10 mg/L to 50 mg/L) and Cu ions (50 mg/L to 500 mg/L) respectively for a time interval of 45 min. UV–vis spectra were taken to determine the amount of adsorbate still present in the solution. To linearly fit the experimental data to the well-known Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, equations (6) and (7) were used.

Where KL (L/mg) stands for the Langmuir constant, KF and nF are known as Freundlich constants respectively, and qmax denotes the maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g).

The FTIR spectrum of Btc-NH-NH2 showed a new band at 3304 cm−1 as compared to parent Btc-Cl which corresponds to the NH2 group, suggesting the formation of Btc-NH-NH2. The peak at 1764 cm−1 in Btc-Cl disappeared in the IR spectrum of Btc-NH-NH2, and a new band at 1647 cm−1 appeared due to the OCNHNH2 group (Fig. 1 a). Therefore, the FTIR spectrum confirmed the formation of the Btc-NH-NH2 ligand from Btc-Cl and was further used to synthesise COF via condensation reaction with terephthaldehyde. As shown in Fig. 1a, the band at 3304 cm−1 corresponds to the –NH2 group in Btc-NH-NH2 disappeared in the IR spectrum of COF after condensation and a new peak at 1546 cm−1 indicating the formation of the –CN group in COF which confirmed the formation of acyl-hydrazone Schiff base linkage from condensation of Btc-NH-NH2 and terephthaldehyde. The FTIR spectrum of COF completely matches all the IR peaks of reported values, demonstrating the formation of COF.[59].

In the FTIR spectra of Zn-MOF (Fig. 1b), two bands may be due to stretching vibrations of the CO bond i.e. asymmetric (ʋ asCOO -) and symmetric stretching (ʋ sCOO -) vibrations at 1602 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 respectively were found. Uncoordinated water molecules’ OH stretching vibrations may cause a peak at 3401 cm−1. The bending and stretching vibrations of the benzene ring and the CO molecule are responsible for the peaks at 868 cm−1 and 1017 cm−1, respectively. Then, the solvothermal process grows Zn-MOF on the surface of COF. The FTIR spectrum of Zn-MOF@COF, a new band of high intensity at 783 cm−1 was observed as compared to parent COF and Zn-MOF which corresponds to Zn-N bond vibrations. This peak showed interactions between the central atom Zn of MOF and the N atom of COF. The SCXRD results also revealed that the Zn-centre has one water molecule in MOF at the axial position that an N atom can easily replace through the coordinate bond. Such types of interactions between MOFs and COFs are reported in the literature.[53] Further, all IR peaks of Zn-MOF (1602, 1402, 1017, 868, 1264 and 1188 cm−1) and COF (1607, 1548, 1247, 947 cm−1) can be all detected in the IR spectra of Zn-MOF@COF composite indicating well growth of Zn-MOF on the surface of COF.

Further, the PXRD spectra of COF revealed microcrystalline material with peaks at 9.4°, 25.1°, 32.2°, and 43.9°, which match the reported values completely. The diffraction peaks for btc-NH-NH2 and terephthaldehyde were not observed in the XRD pattern of COF. Thus, PXRD results confirmed the successful formation of COF. Further, all diffraction peaks of Zn-MOF (2θ = 14.2°, 15.3°, 18.7°, 23.2°, 25.1°) and COF (2θ = 9.4°, 25.1°, 32.2° and 43.9°) can be observed in PXRD pattern of Zn-MOF@COF composite. There is a slight shift in peaks of MOF (2θ = 14.2° and 15.3°) in Zn-MOF@COF, possibly due to interactions between COF and Zn-MOF (Fig. 1c). Thus, PXRD results revealed the successful formation of the Zn-MOF@COF composite.

In addition, the surface area and porous properties of only COF, only Zn-MOF and Zn-MOF@COF composite were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) plot and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, as shown in Fig. 2 . From the Fig. 2a, it is clear that the isotherms resemble the type IV hysteresis loop at the region of high pressure. The surface area for Zn-MOF, COF, and Zn-MOF@COF composites is calculated to be 491.7, 207.7, and 952.9 dm2/g, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 2b, the pore size distribution curve’s solitary, abrupt and highly intense peak revealed the pore width of 1.8, 12.6 and 13.2 nm for Zn-MOF, COF and Zn-MOF@COF composite, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that Zn-MOF@COF is a microporous (2–50 nm) material. The surface area and pore width of the Zn-MOF@COF composite have increased compared to parent Zn-MOF and COF, demonstrating successful coordination between Zn-MOF and COF. Further, the surface morphology of only COF, Zn-MOF, and Zn-MOF@COF composite were analysed using SEM studies. Fig. 3 a and 3b show that Zn-MOF and COF possess particle morphology and large particle size. The SEM image of Zn-MOF@COF (Fig. 3c) shows that the particles of Zn-MOF and COF diffused and exhibited rough surfaces. The surface morphology of Zn-MOF@COF after adsorption of Cu(II) and methylene blue was also analyzed by SEM (Fig. 3d and 3e). The Cu(II) and methylene blue particles have been deposited on Zn-MOF@COF’s surface and the crystallinity of the composite has been reduced up to some extent after the adsorption of Cu(II) and methylene blue.

The chemical state of only Zn-MOF, COF and Zn-MOF@COF composite were analysed using XPS. The characteristic peaks of COF (O1s, N1s and C1s) and Zn-MOF (C1s, O1s, Zn2p) can be detected in XPS spectra of Zn-MOF@COF composite demonstrating successful coordination of Zn-MOF and COF (Fig. 4 a). Three deconvoluted peaks are visible in the C1s spectrum of the Zn-MOF@COF composite, which could be due to the CC, CC/CN, and CO groups, respectively, at 284.6, 285.6, and 288.2 eV(Fig. 4b). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s of Zn-MOF@COF composite resembles C1s spectra of Zn-MOF and COF (Figure S1 & S3). Two deconvoluted peaks, corresponding to the Zn-O and –OH groups, are visible in the O1s spectrum of the Zn-MOF@COF composite at 531.5 and 532.5 eV (Fig. 4e), which is similar to O1s spectra of Zn-MOF and COF (Figure S2 & S4). Furthermore, two peaks at 1022.3 and 1045.4 eV, corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, are visible in the high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn2p of Zn-MOF@COF composite (Fig. 4f), which is similar to Zn2p spectrum of MOF but peak intensity of Zn2p has been reduced in case of Zn-MOF@COF as compared to Zn2p peak in MOF (Fig. 4a). The XPS spectra of N1s of Zn-MOF@COF composite show only one peak at 400.2 eV which corresponds to the CN group (Fig. 4c) while the N1s spectrum of COF shows three peaks at 401.3, 400.01 and 402.29 eV, that can be assigned to the NCO, CN/NH, and N+ , respectively (Fig. 4d). The reduced intensity of the Zn2p peak and disappearance of N1s peaks at 401.3 and 402.29 eV in Zn-MOF@COF composite further demonstrating that there are interactions between Zn atom of Zn-MOF and N atom of COF substantiate successful coordination of Zn-MOF and COF to form Zn-MOF@COF hybrid composite material. In addition, the presence of C, N, O and Zn atoms in Zn-MOF@COF was also confirmed by EDS mapping. As shown in Figure S18, all Zn, C, Nand O atoms are present in Zn-MOF@COF showing the successful formation of hybrid material.

Selective and sensitive response of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+ ions among various heavy metal ions: The Zn-MOF@COF exhibit a strong emission peak at 371 nm upon excitation at 285 nm which can be attributed to π → π* transition (Fig. 1d). The luminescence properties of Zn-MOF@COF arise due to the π-conjugated organic linker’s emission. We sought to explore the efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF in the detection and removal of environmental pollutants such as heavy metal ions and organic dyes. For this purpose, we first evaluated the response of Zn-MOF@COF as chemical sensor for several metal ions including Cd2+, Co3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sb3+, U6+, Zn2+ and Zr4+. The fluorescence spectra of Zn-MOF@COF after the addition of 100 μM concentration of each metal ion were recorded (Fig. 5 a). As depicted in Figure 5a, Cu2+ significantly quenched the emission intensity of Zn-MOF@COF as compared to other metal ions. To test the selective behaviour of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+, the effect of interfering ions on the detection of Cu2+ was estimated. The experiments using interfering metal ions were carried out (Fig. 5c), and the Cu2+-induced emission spectra of Zn-MOF@COF were unaffected by other interfering metal ion species, indicating excellent selectivity of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ ions.

Next, the fluorescence spectra of Zn-MOF@COF upon incremental addition of Cu2+ were recorded (Fig. 5b) and we observed a gradual increase in the fluorescence quenching of Zn-MOF@COF. With an excellent linear fitting of R2 = 0.9849, the Stern-Volmer plot (Fo/F = 1 + Ksv [Cu2+]) demonstrated a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensities of Zn-MOF@COF in the wider concentration ranges of Cu2+ (0–75 μM). Furthermore, the strong interactions between Cu2+ and Zn-MOF@COF were highlighted by the quenching constant (Ksv) value of 0.7 × 105 M−1. By using the formulas 3σ/m and 5σ/m, respectively, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank readings of Zn-MOF@COF and m is the slope of the calibration curve, the detection limit (LOD) and quantification limit (LOQ) were determined. According to calculations as shown in Figure S13, the LOD and LOQ of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ are 0.15 μM and 0.26 μM, respectively. Notably, among the published sensors, this LOD of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ is the lowest, further substantiating its relevance for Cu2+ detection (table S2). Further, the surface morphology of Zn-MOF@COF after the adsorption of Cu2+ ions was analysed by SEM studies. As can be seen in Fig. 3d, the Cu2+ ions deposit on the surface of Zn-MOF@COF and the crystallinity of Zn-MOF@COF has been reduced after Cu2+ adsorption. Moreover, EDS spectra (Fig. 3f) of Zn-MOF@COF after adsorption of Cu2+ confirmed the presence of Cu2+ in Zn-MOF@COF with 11.82 wt%. In addition, Zeta potential analysis revealed that Zn-MOF@COF possesses an anionic framework with an overall charge of −21.8 mV. This charge has been decreased to −16.4 mV after the adsorption of Cu2+ ions, demonstrating the electron density transfer process from Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+. Further, the effect of pH on the detection of Cu2+ ions by Zn-MOF@COF composite was studied. The fluorescence detection of Cu2+ ions by Zn-MOF@COF was similar at pH ranges from 2 to 14 (Figure S12). Therefore, Zn-MOF@COF can be a chemosensor for Cu2+ ions at different pH.

Monitoring of Cu(II) levels by Zn-MOF@COF in real water samples: To determine if Zn-MOF@COF is suitable for the detection of Cu2+ in true samples, the luminescence spectra of Zn-MOF@COF in actual specimens injected with targeted metallic ions were captured. Real water samples from various areas in India were gathered for this experiment, including lake water from the Sukhna Lake in Chandigarh, groundwater from the Doab region in Punjab, and pond water from Panjab University in Chandigarh. Cu2+ ions at 5 μM, 10 μM, and 15 μM were added to the water samples. As shown in Table 1 , a high recovery rate for Cu2+ ions was noted. The findings suggested that Cu2+ detection by Zn-MOF@COF in actual watersamples is in adequate agreement with the primary samples. Hence, Zn-MOF@COF can successfully detect Cu2+ ions in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Cu(II) ions and methylene blue by Zn-MOF@COF from water
ce:section.ce:para: Dosage Study of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: Adsorbent dosage is a major parameter that can affect the adsorption efficiency of an adsorbent. The effect of Zn-MOF@COF dosage on the adsorption capacities and removal efficiencies of MB and Cu2+ ions was investigated to establish the ideal dosage of Zn-MOF@COF. To perform this experiment, various amounts of Zn-MOF@COF (0.1 to 0.6 g/L for Cu2+ ions and 0.2 to 1.2 g/L for MB dye) were added to the fixed concentration of MB (5 ml, 10 ppm) and Cu2+ ions (5 ml, 50 ppm). The mixture was stirred for an optimum time to attain the maximum adsorption of the adsorbate. The percentage removal of Cu2+ ions was monitored via ICP-MS results and that of MB was calculated with the help of absorbance spectra using equation no. 1. According to the results, the maximum adsorption was attained with 0.4 g/L and 1 g/L amount of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ ions and MB respectively. A saturation in removal percentage was obtained after this optimum dosage of Zn-MOF@COF as shown in Figures S6, S7 and S8. In general, increasing the amount of adsorbent used, causes the proportion of adsorbate ions to the vacant active sites of the adsorbent to decrease, thereby enhancing the removal efficiency.[51] At starting, the gradual increase in the removal capacity with the dosage of Zn-MOF@COF was due to the increased proportion of active sites to the adsorbate ions. Further increase in the amount of Zn-MOF@COF may result in the aggregation of adsorbent particles hence decreasing the availability of the vacant sites leading to the saturation in removal percentage.[3] Based on the above results, 0.4 g/L and 1 g/L dosages of Zn-MOF@COF were fixed as the optimum amounts for Cu2+ ions and MB respectively.

As suggested by the reviewer, the effect of pH on the adsorption process of Zn-MOF@COF towards MB was investigated in the pH range of 2 to 14. Meanwhile, in the adsorption process of Cu2+, the impact of pH was analysed within the range of 2.0 to 7.0 to mitigate the impact of precipitation of copper ions forming Cu(OH)2 that may occur at higher pH values. At lower pH levels, protonation of the sorption sites occurs. The elevated concentration of H+ ions may increase the positive charge on the Zn-MOF@COF surface, resulting in electrostatic repulsion and, consequently, lower adsorption efficiency. As the pH rises from 2.0 to 7.0, the concentration of H+ ions in the medium decreases, facilitating the availability of sorption sites for positively charged Cu2+ ions and cationic MB dye. The decrease in adsorption efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF towards MB at a fundamental medium was due to the interaction of cationic dye with OH– ions, resulting in the decline in electrostatic interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent. Additionally, a highly acidic medium's relatively modest adsorption capacity could be attributed to the competition between numerous H+ ions and Cu2+ ions. As the solution pH rises, the uptake capacities of Cu2+ions exhibit a pronounced increase, reaching their maximum at pH = 6.0 for Cu2+ and pH = 8 for MB. The maximum percentage uptake of Cu2+ ions and MB was observed at pH 6 and 8, respectively.

Kinetics of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: The change in the adsorption potential of Zn-MOF@COF towards MB and Cu2+ ions concerning agitation time is displayed in Fig. 6 a & 6b. It can be seen that an instant enhancement in the removal capacity of Cu2+ ions will take place by increasing the time from 0 to 10 min, which is due to the presence of a large number of vacant adsorption sites at an initial stage. The approximate saturation in the adsorption process was observed after 35 min of the experiment. Figure S9 displayed the effect of agitation time on the removal efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF and its components towards MB dye. Among all, the synthesised composite material showed the highest decrease in the absorbance peak of MB. In this case, a gradual increase in the adsorption efficiency was noticed with time and an equilibrium was achieved within 45 min. Moreover, selecting the right model for data analysis is crucial for gaining additional insights into the reaction pathway. As a result, various kinetic models, including Pseudo-First-Order, Pseudo-Second-Order, and intraparticle diffusion models, were used to analyse the experimental results (Fig. 6-c, d, e, f). The data obtained from each model, including the values of rate constants (K), correlation coefficients (R2), and adsorption efficiency at equilibrium, are summarized in Table 2 . To determine the most suited model to fit the experimental data, the R2 values and the deviance of calculated qe values (obtained via linear plots of the kinetic models) from experimental ones must be considered.[3] The results revealed that the pseudo-second-order model is best fitted with a higher R2 value than the pseudo-first-order model. Moreover, the calculated qe value for Pseudo-Second-Order was found close to the experimental value, confirming the satisfaction of this model. Applying the Pseudo-Second-Order model in data fitting suggests the involvement of chemical forces during the adsorption process of Cu(II) and MB. The electron transfer between adsorbent functional groups and adsorbate ions occurring in the adsorption process contributes to the observed favourable fit with the Pseudo-Second-Order model, which was also confirmed by Zeta-potential analysis. This interaction underscores the significance of chemical forces in governing the adsorption behaviour via the chemisorption mechanism.

Figures S10 and S11 show the intraparticle diffusion model for the adsorption of MB and Cu2+. According to this model, if the linear straight line crosses the origin, the adsorption is primarily regulated by intraparticle diffusion; otherwise, it is governed by multiple processes.[60] Herein, the model line does not pass through the origin, resulting in a non-zero intercept; hence, in this case, the intraparticle diffusion model does not control the adsorption process.

Isotherm study of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: The isotherms of adsorption for Cu2+ and MB have been examined at different initial concentrations to figure out the highest removal efficiencies and the impact of the initial amount of adsorbate on the adsorptionbehaviourofadsorbent. The results of the isotherm are depicted in Fig. 7 a and 7b. Initially, an instant enhancement in the adsorption capacity was observed for Cu2+, whereas MB shows a gradual rise in the qe value on moving from a lower to a higher concentration of adsorbate. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were also used to analyse the gathered data. The findings are displayed in Fig. 7 (c, d, e, and f), and Table 3 includes the estimated values. Both Cu2+ and MB showed different adsorption isotherm behaviour. The experimental data of Cu2+ adsorption fits more accurately in the Langmuir isotherm model as compared to Freundlich due to a higher R2 value of 0.999. It implies that the adsorption of Cu2+ onto Zn-MOF@COF is a monolayer on a homogeneous surface without any interaction between adsorbate ions.[51] The existence of monolayers implies a chemical bonding between pollutants and the surface of Zn-MOF@COF adsorbent. The presence of oxygen functional groups on the material's surface may facilitate processes such as electron transfer or surface complexation, ultimately giving rise to the development of chemical bonds with adsorbates, which was further supported by zeta potential analysis, which exhibited a change in potential after adsorption of adsorbates. In the case of MB, the Freundlich isotherm model matches the experimental results with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.997) depicting the multilayer adsorption.[30] The remarkable adsorption capacity exhibited by Zn-MOF@COF arises from the expansive specific surface area of Zn-MOF coupled with the distinctive properties inherent in COF. This combination positions it as an exceptional adsorbent for efficiently removing and recovering heavy metals, contributing significantly to wastewater treatment applications.

The estimated values of maximum adsorption efficiency of Zn-MOF@COF towards Cu2+ and MB is 500 mg/g and 26.3 mg/g, respectively. Moreover, the 1/nF and RL values stand between 0 and 1, which indicates a favourable adsorption process.[3].

Thermodynamic Study of Zn-MOF@COF for adsorption of Cu(II) and MB: To assess a process's spontaneity, adsorption mechanism, or heat change, it is crucial to determine key thermodynamic parameters like ΔH°, ΔS° and ΔG°. The values of ΔS° and ΔH° were calculated with the help of intercept and slope obtained from the linear plot between lnqe/Ce and T1/2 in a temperature range of 15 to 55 °C using equation (8). Meanwhile, the value of ΔG° could be measured using equation (9).

Where, ΔH°, ΔS° and ΔG° are the standard enthalpy (KJ mol−1), entropy (KJ mol−1) and Gibbs free energy (KJ mol−1) of the adsorption process. R is the universal gas constant (j k−1 mol−1) and T corresponds to temperature in kelvin.

Fig. 8 a represents the linear Van’t Hoff plot with the acceptable value of correlation coefficients (R2 = 0.996 and 0.979). The calculated values of all the essential thermodynamic parameters are shown in Table S1. The positive value of ΔH° suggests that the temperature is directly proportional to the adsorption rate; hence, the reaction process favoured the endothermic nature.[4] Moreover, the negative value of ΔS° indicates the decrease in randomness and increase in the order of adsorbent on the solid/liquid interface due to the interaction between Zn-MOF@COF and adsorbents. The value of ΔG° is a deciding factor in whether the reaction is spontaneous or requires energy. The positive ΔG° value expressed the non-spontaneous behaviour of the process, indicating that the reaction requires continuous stirring.[61].

The recyclability of any chemosensor makes it a potential candidate for the removal of pollutants from the environment. In this perspective, we sought to explore the regeneration ability of Zn-MOF@COF for its practical utility. Therefore, Zn-MOF@COF could be regenerated by using methanol to wash out the adsorbate i.e. MB and Cu2+. Zn-MOF@COF was successfully restored for subsequent adsorption after being utilised for the initial elution of Cu2+ and MB by washing the adsorbent with methanol. As illustrated in Fig. 8, There is a slight difference between the regeneration ability of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ and MB. This could be explained on the basis of adsorption isotherm data. The adsorption process of Cu2+ followed a monolayer pattern, whereas MB exhibited multilayered adsorption behaviour. Removing the adsorbate with monolayered adsorption is pretty easy compared to multilayered. Hence, this could be the reason for better regeneration properties of Zn-MOF@COF for Cu2+ than MB.

XPS analysis was utilized to examine the changes in the state of MOF@COF before and after adsorbates were introduced. Figure S20 illustrates the spectra of MOF@COF subsequent to after addition of Cu2+ and MB. Following the introduction of adsorbates, a minor shift in the binding energy of O1s was detected, indicating the successful binding of Cu2+ and MB to the available oxygen atoms of the carboxylate linker as depicted in Figure S21 and S22. The presence of copper ions in MOF@COF was confirmed by two deconvoluted peaks at 955.2 eV and 935.2 eV, attributed to Cu2p, signifying CuO interactions upon immersion in a Cu2+ solution. These signals, denoted as Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 at 711.22 eV and 725.08 eV respectively, were accompanied by satellite peaks at 944 eV, affirming the stable +2 oxidation state of Cu. Furthermore, the peaks of Zn2p with binding energies of 1022.3 eV and 1045 eV persisted after the adsorption of Cu2+ and MB, suggesting the preservation of the MOF@COF structure under these conditions.

In summary, an interlinked hybrid composite material Zn-MOF@COF from Zn-based MOF and imine-based COF is designed in this work. The Zn-N coordination bond plays a significant role in synthesising interlinked Zn-MOF@COF composite material, which could be determined well based on experimental analysis. The hybrid composite's mesoporous framework and particle-sized morphology endow it with excellent properties for decontaminating Cu2+ ions and MB from water. As a proof-of-concept applicability in the field of sensors, the hybrid composite Zn-MOF@COF show 99.8 and 97.6 % adsorption efficiency for Cu2+ and MB, respectively, with low detection limit and high quenching constant for Cu2+ ions. The detection and removal performance of Zn-MOF@COF exceeds most of the available state-of-the-art MOF and COF-based materials. The Zn-MOF@COF-based hybrid composite materials were explored for energy storage devices but rarely explored in the literature for the removal of water pollutants. The present work provides novel approaches for combining COFs and Zn-MOFs in a single system to improve structural and chemical stability for enhanced sensing efficiency.
